stories.body: Families in La Guaira accuse authorities of negligence after last week’s twin earthquakes killed at least 1,700 people, saying rescuers arrived days late and many neighborhoods still lack search teams even as volunteers dig through collapsed government housing blocks; Monday’s 4.6-magnitude aftershock reawakened panic in Caracas, forced metro closures and briefly halted rescue work, though international crews and U.S. naval aid are now operating through the repaired port as the UN warns the death toll could climb far higher.
stories.headline: Venezuelans decry slow relief as aftershocks rattle quake zone

stories.body: U.S. forces struck Iranian coastal radar sites after downing drones over the Strait of Hormuz, while Reuters reports Iran is taking a stern line on a proposed interim deal that would swap sanctions relief for reopening the waterway and calming fronts in Lebanon, even as President Donald Trump claims Tehran requested talks in Doha that Iranian officials deny; amid the stalemate Iran insists any broader peace requires an Israel-Hezbollah ceasefire and access to frozen oil revenues, and Washington faces pressure to curb energy prices without conceding on Iran’s nuclear program.
stories.headline: U.S.-Iran conflict festers as Doha talks remain uncertain

stories.body: Iranian officials reaffirm that any deal with Washington must include an Israeli withdrawal from southern Lebanon, backing Hezbollah as clashes continue despite overlapping ceasefire texts, while The Guardian reports the 14-point memorandum on reopening Hormuz is unraveling because vague language on Lebanon and shipping has fueled accusations of bad faith, and Al Jazeera notes new hostilities and maritime skirmishes have already placed the recent understanding at risk.
stories.headline: Lebanon ceasefire dispute jeopardizes Iran-U.S. memorandum

stories.body: A crack-induced air leak in Russia’s Zvezda module doubled the station’s air loss rate and led NASA to order five astronauts into their Crew Dragon spacecraft for two hours before returning them to the ISS once Roscosmos paused repair efforts, highlighting rare safe-haven procedures and ongoing disagreements over how to fix the aging service module as lawmakers push to extend the station’s life to 2032.
stories.headline: NASA rescinds ISS evacuation order after air leak scare

stories.body: Heavy missile barrages set buildings, cars and infrastructure ablaze across Kyiv, prompting officials to warn residents to stay in shelters while President Volodymyr Zelenskyy cautioned that intelligence points to another massive strike amid Russian threats of systematic attacks on decision centers; simultaneously, Al Jazeera reports Moscow rejected a purported Ukrainian proposal to limit long-range strikes even as Kyiv expanded drone attacks on Russian refineries, underscoring escalating pressure on both sides.
stories.headline: Kyiv braces for major assault as Russian strikes ignite fires

stories.body: Ousted prime minister Ousmane Sonko says his Pastef party, which holds a parliamentary majority, will refuse to join President Bassirou Diomaye Faye’s reshuffled government after disagreements over the party’s executive role, deepening political turmoil just as Senegal seeks to unfreeze a $1.8 billion IMF program suspended when misreported debt pushed liabilities to 132 percent of GDP.
stories.headline: Sonko’s Pastef shuns new Senegalese government amid debt crunch

stories.body: In a 52-47 vote Senate Republicans approved $70 billion in additional immigration enforcement funding for ICE and Border Patrol, rejected Democratic efforts to permanently kill Donald Trump’s $1.8 billion “anti-weaponization” restitution fund and brushed aside amendments targeting a proposed White House ballroom, leaving House consideration for next week even as several GOP senators in tight races voiced unease about defending the fund before November.
stories.headline: Senate passes $70 billion ICE boost while Trump restitution fund survives

stories.body: Forty-seven million people from the Midwest into the Ohio Valley are under extreme heat warnings with temperatures in the 90s Fahrenheit and heat indices above 100, prompting cities such as Nashville and Des Moines to close camps, open cooling centers and dispatch workers for wellness checks as New York readies for similar conditions later in the week.
stories.headline: Severe heat wave forces cancellations across Midwest and East

stories.body: The Snyder Fire straddling the Colorado-Utah border burned through tinder-dry forests under unprecedented conditions, trapping and killing three firefighters who reportedly tried to shield themselves from flames, prompting solemn processions in Grand Junction while authorities compared the tragedy to Arizona’s deadly 2013 Yarnell Hill blaze as crews continue fighting the nation’s largest active wildfire.
stories.headline: Three firefighters killed battling Utah-Colorado wildfire

stories.body: The Supreme Court upheld state laws permitting mailed ballots that arrive after Election Day if postmarked on time, preserving rules in nearly 30 states and rebuffing Trump’s push to tighten deadlines, as the justices near term’s end with pending decisions on Trump’s birthright citizenship order, his efforts to fire independent agency heads and a dispute over transgender sports bans, geofence warrants and limits on party spending ahead of the 2026 midterms.
stories.headline: Supreme Court term reshapes election rules and executive power

stories.body: After Vox leader Santiago Abascal accused Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez of trying to steal elections, PP leader Alberto Núñez Feijóo and Madrid president Isabel Díaz Ayuso suggested Spain’s “grandchildren law” granting nationality to descendants of emigrants could be used to manipulate voter rolls, provoking outrage from Spanish diplomats who say the allegations impugn their professionalism without evidence as they manage a surge in applications across Latin America.
stories.headline: Diplomats bristle at PP and Vox fraud claims tied to ‘grandchildren law’

stories.body: National Court judge Santiago Pedraz, acting on an anti-corruption request, has charged SEPI president Belén Gualda and two dozen officials and executives, including former ENUSA head José Vicente Berlanga and Tubos Reunidos leaders, over alleged influence peddling, embezzlement and insider abuse tied to a network led by ex-PSOE fixer Leire Díez that purportedly steered €132.9 million in public aid and contracts in exchange for commissions funneled through companies linked to party figures.
stories.headline: SEPI chief Belén Gualda among 25 charged in influence-peddling probe

stories.body: Spain’s anti-corruption prosecutors reaffirmed their demand for 15-year prison sentences against former interior minister Jorge Fernández Díaz, ex–state secretary Francisco Martínez, former police operations chief Eugenio Pino and commissioner Andrés Gómez Gordo, plus 19 years for retired commissioner José Manuel Villarejo, alleging they orchestrated the 2013 Kitchen operation to steal documents from ex-PP treasurer Luis Bárcenas, while charges against inspectors Fuentes Gago and Díez Sevillano were dropped after private and popular accusers withdrew.
stories.headline: Prosecutors stick to 15-year request in PP ‘Kitchen’ spying trial

stories.body: The government’s two-and-a-half-month extraordinary regularization window closed with more than one million applications—double initial forecasts and surpassing the 2005 record—with roughly 300,000 cases already admitted for provisional work and residence permits; the rush strained consulates, online queues and documentation requirements just as the EU’s new migration pact tightens future humanitarian permits, prompting migrant advocates to request deadline extensions.
stories.headline: Spain’s extraordinary migrant regularization tops one million applications

stories.body: Barcelona will seal off large portions of the Eixample, Sant Martí and Sants-Montjuïc districts for the Tour de France team presentation on July 2, a 19.6 km team time trial from the Fòrum to Montjuïc on July 4, and the stage-two finish on July 5, forcing extensive traffic bans, bus diversions, selective metro closures and Bicing station suspensions while the city urges residents to rely on reinforced metro service and temporary guarded bike parking.
stories.headline: Tour de France Grand Départ to shut major Barcelona corridors July 2-5

stories.body: A cable fire at Endesa’s Collblanc substation triggered repeated blackouts affecting up to 16,781 customers across Barcelona, L’Hospitalet, Esplugues and Cornellà on Monday, leaving residents without elevators or air conditioning for hours overnight during a heat wave before service was progressively restored around 5 a.m., following another pre-dawn substation blaze that had cut power to 87,000 households.
stories.headline: Power outages hit 17,000 customers in Barcelona metro area amid heat

stories.body: Mossos d’Esquadra recovered shell casings on Carrer Mart in Sant Adrià de Besòs after witnesses reported gunfire around 6:15 p.m. and a man later sought treatment for firearm wounds, reigniting fears in La Mina neighborhood where police recently dismantled parts of a feud between entrenched groups but where residents have endured a string of shootings since 2024 despite mass arrests and weapons seizures.
stories.headline: Fresh shooting in La Mina renews concern over Besòs gun violence
